meta.auditor: Claude Code via Figma REST API

token_implementation.narrative: Material UI implements 446 local variables across 8 collections covering color primitives (252), semantic palette (136), spacing (14), typography (35), breakpoints (5), shape (2), and metadata (2). Color and spacing are well tokenized. However, elevation is implemented as 24 legacy effect styles rather than variables, and typography uses a parallel system of 13 text styles alongside 35 typography variables. This dual-track approach means an AI agent must reconcile two sources of truth for elevation and typography values.

alias_chain_integrity.narrative: Within the local file, 58 palette variables alias to material/colors primitives and all 58 resolve cleanly at depth 1. No broken or circular chains exist among local variables. However, 43 alias references point to remote (external) variable collections that cannot be walked from this file alone — their resolution depends on the source library remaining intact. Additionally, spacing (14 vars), typography (35 vars), and breakpoints (5 vars) have zero alias chains — all values are direct, meaning there is no semantic indirection layer for these categories.

token_architecture_depth.narrative: The file implements two of three required layers. material/colors (252 vars) serves as the primitive layer. palette (136 vars) serves as the semantic layer and correctly aliases into material/colors with light/dark mode support. However, there is no component-level token layer — no collection maps component-specific properties (e.g. button/background, input/border) to semantic tokens. Spacing, typography, and breakpoints are single-layer flat collections with no alias architecture. Without a component token layer, an AI agent cannot determine which semantic token applies to which component property without inspecting the component node tree.

primitive_naming.narrative: material/colors uses a consistent slash-separated convention with a defined scale (amber/50 through amber/900, blue/50 through blue/900). This is machine-parseable and follows Material Design conventions. However, spacing tokens use bare integers (1 through 14) with no prefix or unit hint. Typography mixes conventions: some use slash hierarchy (typography/h1) while others use camelCase (fontFamily, fontWeightRegular) or underscore prefixes (_fontSize/1rem). Breakpoints use bare names (xs, sm, md, lg, xl) without a namespace prefix.

component_to_token_binding.narrative: The REST API and component metadata endpoints do not expose which variables are bound to which component properties — this requires either MCP node inspection or a full file read at depth > 1. From the available data, the absence of a component-level token layer (TA-001) means that even if bindings exist at the node level, they point directly to semantic or primitive tokens rather than through a component-specific indirection. The file has 1,034 published components but the binding relationship between these components and the 446 variables cannot be verified from the endpoints used in this audit.

component_description_coverage.narrative: Of 1,034 published components, 664 (64.2%) have no description at all. Of the 370 that do, 356 contain React code snippets (import/export statements) rather than functional descriptions of intent. Zero components have descriptions that explain when to use the component, when not to use it, or what its functional purpose is. The code-snippet descriptions are auto-generated and provide implementation examples but no design guidance. Button (504 variants), Alert (12 variants), and Card (9 variants — only 2 with descriptions) are the most exposed component pages.

naming_convention_consistency.narrative: Within each artifact type, naming is mostly consistent but conventions diverge across types. Variables use slash hierarchy (palette/text/primary, material/colors/blue/500). Styles also use slash hierarchy (elevation/3, typography/h5, breakpoints/lg). Component sets use angle-bracket convention (<Button>, <TextField>). The palette collection uses underscore-prefixed groups for internal state tokens (_states/hover, _states/selected) which is non-standard. The typography collection mixes slash-based names (typography/h1) with camelCase names (fontFamily, fontWeightMedium) in the same collection. One style uses an asterisk prefix (*library/heading) which breaks machine parsing.

web_readiness_gap.narrative: Component variant properties cover visual states (Enabled, Hovered, Focused, Disabled, Error) which map well to CSS pseudo-classes and ARIA states. However, no ARIA role metadata, accessibility annotations, or keyboard interaction specifications are present in the component metadata. The file has no evidence of accessibility documentation frames. Interactive components (Button with 504 variants, Checkbox with 260 variants, TextField with 81 variants) have extensive visual state coverage but no machine-readable mapping to WAI-ARIA roles or properties. An AI agent generating production code would need to infer all accessibility attributes from component names alone.

governance.narrative: The file shows partial governance through consistent page naming conventions (❖ for components, ♢ for instances, ↓ for section dividers) and a clear structural hierarchy (COMPONENTS → INSTANCES → Blocks). Variable collections enforce hiddenFromPublishing on remote collections, preventing accidental exposure. However, there are no machine-readable governance rules, no evidence of linting or naming enforcement, and no contributor guidelines in the file structure. The presence of a 'Toogle button' page (typo for 'Toggle') suggests no automated naming validation.

checks.detail: All component pages use ❖ prefix, instance pages use ♢ prefix, section dividers use ↓ prefix.

checks.description: Naming validation prevents typos and inconsistencies
checks.detail: Page name 'Toogle button' contains a typo. No automated validation detected.
No linting rules, naming constraints, or governance documentation detected in file metadata.
No evidence of role-based access or contributor guidelines in the file structure.

documentation_quality.narrative: Documentation quality is critically low. The 356 components that have descriptions contain only auto-generated React code snippets — no functional intent, no usage guidance, no constraints, no 'when not to use' guidance. The remaining 664 components have no descriptions at all. The 14 non-code descriptions are minimal labels with no design rationale. Style descriptions are almost entirely absent — only 4 of 43 styles have descriptions, all being terse dimension notes on breakpoint grid styles (e.g. 'Max width 1200px'). There is no evidence of documentation frames in the page structure that could supplement the description fields.

findings.description: Elevation values are implemented as 24 legacy effect styles (elevation/1 through elevation/24) rather than Figma Variables. An AI agent cannot resolve elevation values through the variable system — it must query the styles API separately and reconcile two value resolution paths.
evidence: 24 EFFECT styles named elevation/1 through elevation/24
0 elevation variables in any variable collection
Styles have no descriptions explaining their shadow values
findings.recommendation: Migrate elevation values to a dedicated variable collection (e.g. 'elevation') with numeric values. Retain effect styles as consumers if needed but ensure the variable is the source of truth.

findings.description: Typography is implemented as both variables (35 in the typography collection) and legacy text styles (13 TEXT styles). The two systems overlap — typography/h1 exists as both a variable and a text style. This creates ambiguity for an AI agent about which is authoritative.
evidence: 35 typography variables: typography/h1 through typography/overline, fontFamily, fontWeight variants, _fontSize variants
13 TEXT styles: typography/h1 through typography/overline, *library/heading
Naming overlap between variable and style names
findings.recommendation: Designate one system as authoritative. If variables are the future direction, deprecate text styles and document the migration path. If both must coexist, add descriptions to the text styles indicating they defer to the variable system.

findings.description: Spacing (14 vars), typography (35 vars), and breakpoints (5 vars) collections contain no alias chains. All values are direct (hardcoded within the variable). This means there is no semantic indirection for these categories — a change to the underlying scale requires editing each variable individually rather than updating an alias target.
evidence: spacing collection: 14 variables, 0 aliases, all direct float values
typography collection: 35 variables, 0 aliases, all direct string/float values
breakpoints collection: 5 variables, 0 aliases, all direct float values
findings.recommendation: Create primitive collections for spacing and typography scales. Alias the current semantic-level variables to the primitives. This enables scale changes from a single source.

findings.description: 43 alias references point to remote variable collections hosted in external Figma files. These aliases resolve within the Figma editor but cannot be walked or validated from this file's REST API response alone. If the source library changes or is deleted, these aliases will break silently.
evidence: 43 alias references from remote collections (shape, palette, typography, material/colors, spacing, breakpoints, Custom Style)
Remote collection keys reference external files not accessible via this file's variables/local endpoint
findings.recommendation: Document which external libraries are dependency sources. Periodically validate remote alias targets by querying the source file's variables/local endpoint. Consider internalizing critical remote variables if the source library is outside organizational control.

findings.description: No component-level token layer exists. The architecture has two layers — primitive (material/colors, 252 vars) and semantic (palette, 136 vars) — but no collection maps tokens to specific component properties. Without component tokens (e.g. button/background → palette/primary/main), an AI agent cannot determine which semantic token is intended for which component property without inspecting the node tree of every component.
evidence: 8 local variable collections: material/colors (primitive), palette (semantic), spacing, typography, breakpoints, shape, metadata — none scoped to component properties
Palette variables use role-based names (primary/main, text/primary) but are not further aliased at the component level
findings.recommendation: Create a component-level variable collection that aliases semantic tokens to component-specific property names. Start with the six ❖ component pages: button, alert, card, avatar, checkbox, text-field. Example: button/background/default → palette/primary/main.

findings.description: Spacing, typography, and breakpoints are single-layer flat collections with no primitive/semantic separation. Spacing variables (1 through 14) contain direct pixel multiplier values with no alias to a base unit. Typography variables contain direct font values with no alias to a type scale primitive.
evidence: spacing: 14 vars, names '1' through '14', values are direct floats (e.g. 8, 16, 24)
typography: 35 vars, mixed direct strings and floats, 0 aliases
breakpoints: 5 vars, direct float values (e.g. 444, 600, 900, 1200)
findings.recommendation: Introduce primitive collections for spacing-scale and type-scale. Alias the current flat values through them. This enables a single base-unit change to cascade through all spacing values.

findings.description: Spacing tokens use bare integer names ('1', '2', ... '14') with no namespace prefix, unit indicator, or scale label. A parser cannot distinguish spacing/1 from any other numeric token, and the relationship between the name '1' and the value 8px is opaque without external documentation.
findings.recommendation: Rename spacing variables to include a namespace and scale indicator, e.g. spacing/1 (if the multiplier convention is documented) or spacing/8, spacing/16, spacing/24 (if explicit pixel values are preferred).

findings.description: Typography collection mixes three naming conventions in the same collection: slash-hierarchy (typography/h1), camelCase (fontFamily, fontWeightRegular), and underscore-prefix with slash (_fontSize/1rem). A machine parser must handle all three patterns to extract typography tokens.
evidence: Slash names: typography/h1, typography/h2, typography/body1, typography/body2, typography/caption, typography/overline, typography/subtitle1, typography/subtitle2
camelCase names: fontFamily, fontWeightRegular, fontWeightMedium, fontWeightBold
Underscore-prefix names: _fontSize/0.75rem, _fontSize/0.875rem, _fontSize/1rem
findings.recommendation: Standardize on slash-hierarchy for all typography variables. Rename fontFamily → typography/fontFamily, fontWeightRegular → typography/fontWeight/regular, _fontSize/1rem → typography/fontSize/1rem.

findings.description: Breakpoint variables use bare size names (xs, sm, md, lg, xl) with no namespace prefix. While conventional in Material Design, they are not self-describing for a machine parser encountering them outside the breakpoints collection context.
evidence: breakpoints collection: xs, sm, md, lg, xl
findings.recommendation: Add a namespace prefix: breakpoints/xs, breakpoints/sm, etc. This is consistent with the style naming which already uses breakpoints/ prefix.

findings.description: Component-to-token binding cannot be verified from the REST API endpoints used in this audit. The /components endpoint returns component metadata (names, descriptions, variant properties) but not node-level property bindings. A full file read at depth > 1 or MCP node inspection is required to determine whether component fills, strokes, and spacing are bound to variables. The absence of a component-level token layer (TA-001) compounds this — even if bindings exist, they point to semantic or primitive tokens without component-specific indirection.
evidence: REST API /components endpoint returns 1,034 components with metadata only — no fill, stroke, or spacing binding data
No component-level variable collection exists to indicate intended bindings
Full node tree inspection not performed in this audit run
findings.recommendation: Perform a follow-up audit pass using MCP get_design_context on the six ❖ component pages to inspect node-level variable bindings. Create a component-level token layer to make intended bindings explicit and verifiable without node inspection.

findings.description: Button has 504 variants (the largest component set) but zero descriptions. Without descriptions or a component token layer, an AI agent has no metadata path to determine how Button variants relate to the palette or spacing token systems beyond the variant property names (Color, Size, State, Variant).
evidence: <Button> component set: 360 variants, 0 descriptions
<IconButton> component set: 135 variants, 0 descriptions
<LoadingButton> component set: 9 variants, 0 descriptions
All 504 Button-page components lack descriptions
findings.recommendation: Add descriptions to at least the root component set entries (<Button>, <IconButton>, <LoadingButton>) documenting which tokens bind to which variant properties.

findings.description: 664 of 1,034 published components (64.2%) have no description. Every variant of Button (504), Alert (12), and most of Avatar (24 of 72 missing), Card (9 of 11 missing), and Icon (9) lack descriptions entirely. An AI agent using these components has no metadata to determine functional intent, usage constraints, or when-to-use guidance.
evidence: 664/1034 components (64.2%) have empty description field
Button page: 504 components, 0 descriptions
Alert page: 12 components, 0 descriptions
Card page: 11 components, 2 descriptions
Avatar page: 72 components, 48 descriptions
findings.recommendation: Prioritize descriptions for the six ❖ component set root entries first. At minimum, each component set should have a description on the set-level node explaining purpose, usage context, and constraints. Variant-level descriptions are lower priority.

findings.description: Of 370 components with descriptions, 356 (96.2%) contain auto-generated React code snippets rather than functional descriptions. These snippets show import paths and JSX usage examples but provide no information about when to use the component, when not to use it, or what behavior it provides. Example: every TextField variant description begins with 'import TextField from @mui/material/TextField' followed by a JSX render example.
evidence: 356/370 descriptions are React import+render code snippets
0/370 descriptions contain functional intent language (when to use, constraints, accessibility notes)
14/370 descriptions are non-code but minimal labels
findings.recommendation: Replace code-snippet descriptions with functional descriptions. Code examples belong in external documentation — Figma component descriptions should capture design intent, usage rules, and constraints. Example: 'Primary action trigger. Use for form submissions and primary CTAs. Do not use for navigation — use Link instead.'

findings.description: The palette collection uses underscore-prefixed group names (_states/hover, _states/selected, _states/focus, _states/focusVisible) which deviate from the slash-only convention used elsewhere in the same collection (text/primary, primary/main). The underscore prefix appears to indicate 'internal' tokens but this convention is not documented or applied consistently.
evidence: palette variables with underscore prefix: text/_states/hover, text/_states/selected, text/_states/focus, text/_states/focusVisible, primary/_states/hover, primary/_states/selected, primary/_states/focus, primary/_states/focusVisible
117 of 446 local variables contain underscores
findings.recommendation: Replace underscore prefixes with a documented grouping convention using slashes only. Example: text/states/hover instead of text/_states/hover. If the underscore indicates non-public tokens, use hiddenFromPublishing on those variables instead.

findings.description: One text style uses an asterisk prefix (*library/heading) which is inconsistent with all other style names and would break machine parsers expecting alphanumeric-slash patterns.
evidence: Style name: *library/heading (TEXT type)
All other 42 styles use alphanumeric/slash names: elevation/3, typography/h5, breakpoints/lg
findings.recommendation: Rename *library/heading to a standard convention, e.g. library/heading or typography/library-heading. If the asterisk indicates a private/internal style, use hiddenFromPublishing instead.

findings.description: Page name 'Toogle button' contains a typo (should be 'Toggle button'). This indicates an absence of automated naming validation and would cause a component lookup to fail if an agent searches by canonical name.
evidence: Page id 6026:77516, name '     ♢ Toogle button'
findings.recommendation: Rename to '♢ Toggle Button'. Consider implementing a naming linter that checks page and component names against a dictionary.

findings.description: No ARIA role or accessibility metadata is present in any of the 1,034 published component metadata records. Interactive components (Button, Checkbox, TextField, Select, Radio Group) require role, aria-label, aria-describedby, and keyboard interaction specifications for production web implementation. An AI agent generating code from these components must infer all accessibility attributes from component names alone.
evidence: 0/1034 components contain ARIA metadata in descriptions or properties
Interactive component pages: Button (504 variants), Checkbox (260), TextField (81), Autocomplete (9), Radio Group, Select, Slider, Switch — all without accessibility annotations
findings.recommendation: Add ARIA role and keyboard interaction metadata to component set descriptions. At minimum, document: role attribute, required aria-* properties, keyboard interaction pattern (e.g. 'Space/Enter to activate'). Consider using Figma annotations or a structured description format.

findings.description: Component variant properties cover visual states (Enabled, Hovered, Focused, Disabled, Error) which partially map to CSS pseudo-states and ARIA states. However, there is no explicit mapping between Figma variant property values and web state selectors. An agent must infer that 'State=Disabled' maps to aria-disabled=true and [disabled] attribute.
evidence: Variant properties include: State (Enabled, Hovered, Focused, Disabled, Error), Checked, Indeterminate, Active
No metadata maps these to CSS pseudo-classes (:hover, :focus, :disabled) or ARIA states (aria-disabled, aria-checked)
findings.recommendation: Document state-to-web mappings in component set descriptions or in a dedicated metadata variable collection. Example: State=Disabled → [disabled], aria-disabled='true', pointer-events: none.

findings.description: No machine-readable governance rules are present in the file. Naming conventions, token usage constraints, and contribution guidelines are implicit in the file structure but not documented or enforced programmatically. An AI agent cannot validate whether its output conforms to system governance without external documentation.
evidence: No linting rules, naming constraints, or governance metadata detected
Page naming convention (❖, ♢, ↓ prefixes) is consistent but not documented in the file
Variable naming convention exists but is not enforced — evidenced by mixed camelCase/slash/underscore patterns in typography
findings.recommendation: Create a governance metadata collection or documentation page that machine-readably defines: naming conventions, required description format, token usage rules, and contribution process.

findings.description: The typo in page name 'Toogle button' has persisted through publication, indicating no naming validation gate exists in the contribution or publishing workflow.
evidence: Page '♢ Toogle button' (id: 6026:77516) contains a typo that survived library publication
findings.recommendation: Implement a pre-publish naming check. This could be a manual checklist or an automated script that validates page and component names against expected patterns.

findings.description: Remote variable collections are correctly marked hiddenFromPublishing=true, showing intentional access control. However, the relationship between remote and local collections is not documented — which external file is the source, what the dependency chain is, and who owns the remote collections.
evidence: 7 remote collections with hiddenFromPublishing=true: shape (x2), palette, typography, material/colors, spacing, breakpoints, Custom Style
No documentation of remote collection source files or ownership
findings.recommendation: Document remote collection dependencies in a metadata page or variable collection. Include source file key, owner, and update frequency.

findings.description: No component in the file has documentation that captures functional intent. The 356 components with descriptions contain only React code snippets. The remaining 664 have no descriptions. There is no evidence of documentation frames, usage guidelines, or design rationale anywhere in the file's page structure. An AI agent consuming this file has zero natural-language guidance about component purpose or constraints.
evidence: 356/1034 descriptions are code snippets (import/export/JSX)
0/1034 descriptions contain intent language
664/1034 descriptions are empty
43 styles: 4 have descriptions, all terse dimension notes
No documentation-specific pages detected in page structure (67 pages total)
findings.recommendation: Establish a documentation standard for component descriptions: one sentence of functional intent, one sentence of primary use case, one sentence of constraints or anti-patterns. Apply to the six ❖ component sets first, then extend to ♢ instance pages.

data_gaps.description: Component node-level variable bindings could not be inspected. The REST API /components endpoint returns metadata (names, descriptions, variant properties) but not node fills, strokes, or spacing bindings. A full file read at depth > 1 or MCP node inspection is required.
data_gaps.impact: Likely deflates component_to_token_binding — the score of 25 reflects inability to verify bindings, not confirmed absence. Actual binding coverage may be higher if components use variables at the node level.

data_gaps.description: Remote variable collection alias chains could not be walked. 43 alias references point to variables in external Figma files. The REST API variables/local endpoint only returns variables local to this file — remote alias targets cannot be resolved or validated.
data_gaps.impact: Likely deflates alias_chain_integrity — 43 aliases are marked unresolvable but may be fully intact in the source files.

data_gaps.description: Documentation frames and annotation layers could not be inspected. The depth=1 file read returns page names only, not frame-level content. Some design systems store documentation in frames rather than component descriptions.
data_gaps.impact: Potentially deflates documentation_quality — if documentation exists in frames rather than component descriptions, the score of 10 may understate actual coverage.

data_gaps.description: Instance pages (♢) were not deeply inspected. These pages contain composed patterns (Forms, Headings, Navs, Screens) that may reveal additional governance evidence or documentation not visible from the published component metadata alone.
data_gaps.impact: Potentially deflates governance — block pages may contain structured documentation or naming conventions not visible at the API metadata level.
